metadata.summary: When evaluating open-ended outputs, use another LLM as a judge with a structured rubric, JSON output, evidence cited before scores, per-criterion scoring, and bias mitigation.

content.title: LLM-as-Judge — Rubric, Evidence-First, Bias-Mitigated
content.summary: When you cannot use exact-match or unit-test checks (open-ended outputs: summaries, code review, plans, agent transcripts), use another LLM as a judge — but only with a structured rubric, JSON output, evidence cited BEFORE the score, per-criterion scoring, and explicit mitigation of position/verbosity/self-preference/authority bias. "Rate 1-10" prompts are not evals; they are noise generators.

why.p: Galileo's 2026 review of 12 production judge stacks plus Anthropic's eval guidance both show that vanilla "score this 1-10" judges have inter-rater reliability below Cronbach's alpha 0.5, which is worse than coin-flip on borderline cases. Adding a named rubric, requiring evidence quotes from the candidate output before scoring, and randomising candidate order pushes alpha to 0.8+ and removes the four canonical biases. Without this discipline, you ship a judge that simply prefers longer, GPT-4-flavoured responses regardless of correctness.

list.item: Evaluating open-ended agent outputs where exact-match is impossible (summaries, plans, multi-turn dialogue).
Grading code review comments, refactor plans, or other reasoning artifacts.
Pairwise comparison of two agent variants on the same task set.
Building a regression suite for prompt or model changes that touch generation quality.
Output is verifiable deterministically (compiles, passes tests, matches schema) — judges add cost and noise; use the deterministic check first.
Single-LLM stack on borderline cases — self-preference bias is unfixable; bring in a second model family for cross-judging.
Privacy-sensitive outputs you cannot send to a third-party judge — keep evals local or skip.
Throwaway prototypes — overhead of rubric design is not justified before the agent is real.

section.name: required-rubric-shape
section.p: A judge prompt is only useful when it forces named criteria, evidence quotes from the candidate output, and per-criterion scoring inside a strict JSON schema.
A rubric has four moving parts: named criteria with weights, scale definition with anchors, output schema that requires evidence before scoring, and a per-criterion rationale.

rule.statement: Every judge prompt must require structured JSON output with: per-criterion evidence (verbatim quote from candidate), per-criterion score on a fixed scale, per-criterion rationale, and a final weighted total — in that order.
rule.rationale: Forcing the judge to quote evidence BEFORE emitting a score binds the score to specific text, which is what raises Cronbach's alpha from ~0.45 (1-10 prompts) to ~0.85 across studies summarised by Adnan Masood (2024) and Langfuse's 2026 evaluation guide.

example.note: Evidence is quoted from the candidate, scores reference fixed criteria, total is computed not guessed.
No criteria, no evidence, no rationale — judge has nothing to anchor on, so output is pure narrative drift.

antipattern.description: "Rate this response 1-10" with no rubric, no evidence requirement, no JSON.
antipattern.reason: Output becomes a function of judge mood, candidate length, and surface fluency rather than the criteria you actually care about.

rule.statement: Run the judge prompt on the same candidate set 3-5 times with shuffled candidate order; compute Cronbach's alpha across runs. Reject the judge if alpha < 0.7.
rule.rationale: An unstable judge can never be a reliable eval signal. Stability is a precondition, not a nice-to-have, per Galileo's 2026 framework.

section.name: mitigating-the-four-canonical-biases
section.p: Position, verbosity, self-preference, and authority biases each have a known mechanism and a known counter; ship none of the counters and your judge becomes a length-and-style detector.

list.item: Position bias: judge prefers whichever candidate is shown first (or last) in pairwise prompts.
Verbosity bias: longer answers score higher regardless of correctness.
Self-preference: a model judging its own family's outputs scores them ~5-15% higher.
Authority bias: the judge defers to confident-sounding language even when factually wrong.

rule.statement: For pairwise judging, randomise candidate order per task AND run each pair twice with positions swapped; final verdict requires agreement across both orderings — disagreements count as ties.
rule.rationale: Direct measurement by Arize (2024-2026) shows position-swap reduces Order Bias by 60-90%; tie-on-disagree prevents the judge from breaking arbitrary ties as if they were meaningful.

example.note: Two orderings, agreement required — kills position bias on the metric you actually report.

example.code: def judge_pair(a, b, judge): return judge(a, b).winner # one ordering, no swap
example.note: Whichever candidate sits first wins ~57% of borderline cases regardless of merit.

rule.statement: Normalise candidate length before judging: truncate to a common token cap, OR add explicit instruction "ignore length differences; longer is not better".
rule.rationale: Verbosity bias is consistently 10-30% on raw prompts (Arize 2026 LLM-judge writeup); normalisation drops it to under 5%.

rule.statement: Cross-judge: never use a single model family as the only judge for outputs from that same family. Use at least two different families (e.g., Claude judges GPT outputs, GPT judges Claude outputs) and compare.
rule.rationale: Self-preference is well-documented (Zheng et al. MT-bench, replicated across 2024-2026 evaluations). Cross-family judging exposes the bias and lets you flag tasks where only the in-family judge "agrees".

antipattern.description: Single-judge production setup with no order swap, no length normalisation, no cross-model verification.
antipattern.reason: Looks rigorous because there is JSON output and a rubric, but the four biases combine to make the metric a measurement of style, not quality.

template.purpose: Reusable rubric template with criteria, weights, output schema, and judge-prompt slots.
Plain-text judge prompt with bias-mitigation instructions and evidence-first ordering.

see-also.link: Anthropic eval guidance — outcome plus rubric for open-ended outputs.
Arize 2026 catalog of LLM-judge biases and counters.
Galileo 2026 — bias mitigation as part of judge readiness.
